patterns.description: In Python 2, bytes[i] returns a str of length 1, so ord(c) is required. In Python 3, bytes[i] returns int directly, making ord(int) a TypeError.
patterns.python2_behavior: bytes[i] returns str of length 1; ord(c) returns int — correct
patterns.python3_behavior: bytes[i] returns int; ord(int) raises TypeError: ord() expected string of length 1, but int found
patterns.example_bug: c = data[0]; val = ord(c)  # crashes in Python 3 if data is bytes

patterns.description: In Python 2, chr() returns a str (bytes). In Python 3, chr() returns a unicode str. Binary write operations expecting bytes receive str instead.
patterns.python2_behavior: chr(65) returns b'A' (1-byte str) — valid for binary output
patterns.python3_behavior: chr(65) returns 'A' (unicode str) — TypeError when concatenated with bytes or written to binary stream
patterns.example_bug: buf += chr(0x00)  # TypeError: can only concatenate str to str, not bytes

patterns.description: Function returns empty str literal but also returns bytes in other branches. In Python 2 str == bytes so mixing was silent. In Python 3 returns inconsistent type.
patterns.python2_behavior: return '' and return b'data' have the same type — str
patterns.python3_behavior: return '' is str; return b'data' is bytes — caller receives inconsistent types causing downstream TypeError
patterns.example_bug: def read_header(): return b'' if data else ''  # mixed return types

patterns.description: bytes.find(str) always returns -1 in Python 3 with no error. In Python 2, str.find(str) worked because bytes == str.
patterns.python2_behavior: data.find('\n') on a str (bytes) finds the newline — returns index
patterns.python3_behavior: data.find('\n') on bytes always returns -1 silently — logic error, no crash
patterns.example_bug: pos = data.find('\r\n')  # always -1 when data is bytes in Python 3

patterns.description: bytes.split(str) raises TypeError in Python 3. In Python 2, str.split(str) was valid because bytes == str.
patterns.python2_behavior: data.split('\n') on str (bytes) splits on newline — returns list of str
patterns.python3_behavior: data.split('\n') on bytes raises TypeError: a bytes-like object is required, not 'str'
patterns.example_bug: lines = raw_data.split('\n')  # TypeError when raw_data is bytes

patterns.description: bytes.startswith(str) always returns False in Python 3 with no error. Silent logic failure.
patterns.python2_behavior: data.startswith('GET') on str (bytes) returns True if data begins with those bytes
patterns.python3_behavior: data.startswith('GET') on bytes always returns False — silent logic error
patterns.example_bug: if line.startswith('HTTP/'):  # always False when line is bytes

patterns.description: bytes.endswith(str) always returns False in Python 3 with no error. Silent logic failure.
patterns.python2_behavior: data.endswith('\r\n') on str (bytes) returns True if data ends with CRLF
patterns.python3_behavior: data.endswith('\r\n') on bytes always returns False — silent logic error
patterns.example_bug: if response.endswith('\r\n'):  # always False when response is bytes

patterns.description: Comparing bytes to a str literal always returns False in Python 3 with no error. The most dangerous pattern — no crash, pure silent logic failure.
patterns.python2_behavior: b'OK' == 'OK' is True — bytes and str were the same type
patterns.python3_behavior: b'OK' == 'OK' is always False — different types, no TypeError, silent incorrect logic
patterns.example_bug: if status == 'OK':  # always False when status is bytes from socket

patterns.description: dict.has_key() was removed in Python 3. Use 'key in dict' instead.
patterns.python2_behavior: d.has_key('x') returns True if 'x' is a key — works
patterns.python3_behavior: AttributeError: 'dict' object has no attribute 'has_key'
patterns.example_bug: if headers.has_key('Content-Type'):  # AttributeError in Python 3

patterns.description: basestring was the common base class of str and unicode in Python 2. Removed in Python 3.
patterns.python2_behavior: isinstance(x, basestring) checks for any string type — works
patterns.python3_behavior: NameError: name 'basestring' is not defined
patterns.example_bug: if isinstance(val, basestring):  # NameError in Python 3

patterns.description: unicode() builtin was removed in Python 3. str() is the unicode string type.
patterns.python2_behavior: unicode(x) returns unicode string representation of x
patterns.python3_behavior: NameError: name 'unicode' is not defined
patterns.example_bug: url = unicode(url) if is_py2 else str(url)  # NameError if guard fails

patterns.description: xrange() was removed in Python 3. range() returns a lazy iterator in Python 3 equivalent to xrange().
patterns.python2_behavior: xrange(n) returns a lazy iterator — memory efficient
patterns.python3_behavior: NameError: name 'xrange' is not defined
patterns.example_bug: for i in xrange(len(data)):  # NameError in Python 3

patterns.description: raw_input() was renamed to input() in Python 3. Python 2 input() evaluated the expression.
patterns.python2_behavior: raw_input(prompt) reads a line from stdin as str
patterns.python3_behavior: NameError: name 'raw_input' is not defined
patterns.example_bug: answer = raw_input('Continue? ')  # NameError in Python 3

patterns.description: reduce() was moved from builtins to functools in Python 3.
patterns.python2_behavior: reduce(fn, iterable) works as a builtin
patterns.python3_behavior: NameError: name 'reduce' is not defined (unless imported from functools)
patterns.example_bug: total = reduce(lambda a, b: a + b, values)  # NameError in Python 3

patterns.description: filter() returns a list in Python 2 but a lazy iterator in Python 3. Code that indexes or slices the result breaks.
patterns.python2_behavior: filter(fn, lst) returns a list — indexable, has len()
patterns.python3_behavior: filter(fn, lst) returns a filter object — TypeError on indexing or len()
patterns.example_bug: result = filter(None, items)[0]  # TypeError: 'filter' object is not subscriptable

patterns.description: map() returns a list in Python 2 but a lazy iterator in Python 3. Code that indexes or iterates twice breaks.
patterns.python2_behavior: map(fn, lst) returns a list — reusable, indexable
patterns.python3_behavior: map(fn, lst) returns a map object — exhausted after first iteration, TypeError on indexing
patterns.example_bug: result = map(int, values); print(result[0])  # TypeError in Python 3

patterns.description: zip() returns a list of tuples in Python 2 but a lazy iterator in Python 3.
patterns.python2_behavior: zip(a, b) returns a list of tuples — reusable
patterns.python3_behavior: zip(a, b) returns a zip object — exhausted after first iteration
patterns.example_bug: pairs = zip(keys, vals); len(pairs)  # TypeError: object of type 'zip' has no len()

patterns.description: dict.keys() returns a list in Python 2 but a view object in Python 3. Indexing a view raises TypeError.
patterns.python2_behavior: d.keys() returns a list — indexable, sortable by default
patterns.python3_behavior: d.keys() returns a dict_keys view — TypeError on indexing; use list(d.keys())
patterns.example_bug: first_key = d.keys()[0]  # TypeError: 'dict_keys' object is not subscriptable

patterns.description: dict.values() returns a list in Python 2 but a view object in Python 3.
patterns.python2_behavior: d.values() returns a list — indexable
patterns.python3_behavior: d.values() returns a dict_values view — TypeError on indexing
patterns.example_bug: vals = d.values(); vals.sort()  # AttributeError: 'dict_values' object has no attribute 'sort'

patterns.description: dict.items() returns a list of tuples in Python 2 but a view in Python 3.
patterns.python2_behavior: d.items() returns a list of (key, value) tuples
patterns.python3_behavior: d.items() returns a dict_items view — TypeError on indexing
patterns.example_bug: first = d.items()[0]  # TypeError: 'dict_items' object is not subscriptable

patterns.description: The / operator performs integer division in Python 2 when both operands are int. In Python 3, / always returns float. Use // for integer division.
patterns.python2_behavior: 5 / 2 returns 2 (integer division)
patterns.python3_behavior: 5 / 2 returns 2.5 (float division) — logic errors in size/offset calculations
patterns.example_bug: chunk_size = total / num_chunks  # returns float in Python 3, breaks array indexing

patterns.description: sys.maxint was removed in Python 3. Use sys.maxsize instead.
patterns.python2_behavior: sys.maxint returns the maximum integer value for the platform
patterns.python3_behavior: AttributeError: module 'sys' has no attribute 'maxint'
patterns.example_bug: MAX = sys.maxint  # AttributeError in Python 3

patterns.description: In Python 2, raise Exception, 'message' was valid syntax. In Python 3 only raise Exception('message') is valid.
patterns.python2_behavior: raise ValueError, 'bad value' raises ValueError with message
patterns.python3_behavior: SyntaxError — comma syntax for raise was removed
patterns.example_bug: raise ValueError, 'invalid input'  # SyntaxError in Python 3

patterns.description: Python 2 except clause used comma syntax. Python 3 requires 'as' keyword.
patterns.python2_behavior: except ValueError, e: catches ValueError and binds to e
patterns.python3_behavior: SyntaxError — use except ValueError as e: instead
patterns.example_bug: except IOError, e:  # SyntaxError in Python 3

patterns.description: Python 2 used 'raise ExcType, value, tb' to reraise with traceback. Python 3 uses exc.with_traceback(tb).
patterns.python2_behavior: raise ExcType, value, traceback reraises preserving traceback
patterns.python3_behavior: SyntaxError for old form; with_traceback() is the Python 3 idiom
patterns.example_bug: raise exc_type, exc_value, exc_tb  # SyntaxError in Python 3

patterns.description: Exception chaining and reraise idioms changed between Python 2 and 3. Python 3 uses implicit exception chaining.
patterns.python2_behavior: exc_info = sys.exc_info(); raise exc_info[0], exc_info[1], exc_info[2]
patterns.python3_behavior: SyntaxError for three-argument raise; use 'raise' bare or 'raise X from Y'
patterns.example_bug: t, v, tb = sys.exc_info(); raise t, v, tb  # SyntaxError in Python 3

patterns.description: urllib2 was reorganized into urllib.request and urllib.error in Python 3.
patterns.python2_behavior: import urllib2; urllib2.urlopen() works
patterns.python3_behavior: ImportError: No module named 'urllib2'
patterns.example_bug: import urllib2; response = urllib2.urlopen(url)  # ImportError in Python 3

patterns.description: httplib was renamed to http.client in Python 3.
patterns.python2_behavior: import httplib; httplib.HTTPConnection works
patterns.python3_behavior: ImportError: No module named 'httplib'
patterns.example_bug: import httplib; conn = httplib.HTTPConnection(host)  # ImportError in Python 3

patterns.description: cookielib was renamed to http.cookiejar in Python 3.
patterns.python2_behavior: import cookielib; cookielib.CookieJar() works
patterns.python3_behavior: ImportError: No module named 'cookielib'
patterns.example_bug: import cookielib; jar = cookielib.CookieJar()  # ImportError in Python 3

patterns.description: StringIO module was merged into io in Python 3. StringIO.StringIO becomes io.StringIO.
patterns.python2_behavior: from StringIO import StringIO works
patterns.python3_behavior: ImportError: No module named 'StringIO'; use from io import StringIO
patterns.example_bug: from StringIO import StringIO  # ImportError in Python 3

patterns.description: cPickle was merged into pickle in Python 3 (C implementation is automatic).
patterns.python2_behavior: import cPickle; cPickle.loads() uses fast C implementation
patterns.python3_behavior: ImportError: No module named 'cPickle'; use pickle directly
patterns.example_bug: import cPickle as pickle  # ImportError in Python 3

patterns.description: ConfigParser was renamed to configparser (lowercase) in Python 3.
patterns.python2_behavior: import ConfigParser; ConfigParser.SafeConfigParser() works
patterns.python3_behavior: ImportError: No module named 'ConfigParser'
patterns.example_bug: import ConfigParser; cfg = ConfigParser.ConfigParser()  # ImportError in Python 3

patterns.description: HTMLParser was moved to html.parser in Python 3.
patterns.python2_behavior: import HTMLParser; HTMLParser.HTMLParser() works
patterns.python3_behavior: ImportError: No module named 'HTMLParser'
patterns.example_bug: import HTMLParser; parser = HTMLParser.HTMLParser()  # ImportError in Python 3

patterns.description: Informational pattern. Presence of __future__ imports signals the file was written for dual Python 2/3 compatibility and may contain compat shims worth inspecting.
patterns.python2_behavior: from __future__ import print_function, division etc. opt into Python 3 behavior
patterns.python3_behavior: __future__ imports are no-ops in Python 3 — but their presence marks dual-version code
patterns.example_bug: Informational only — inspect surrounding code for compat shims that may have been incompletely removed

patterns.description: str.encode() with no argument uses the default encoding. In Python 2 the default was ASCII. In Python 3 the default is UTF-8. If the string contains characters in range 128-255, behavior differs.
patterns.python2_behavior: s.encode() uses ASCII by default — raises UnicodeDecodeError on non-ASCII
patterns.python3_behavior: s.encode() uses UTF-8 by default — silently produces different bytes for non-ASCII
patterns.example_bug: key = username.encode()  # produces different bytes for non-ASCII usernames

patterns.description: bytes.decode() with no encoding argument uses UTF-8. If bytes contain non-UTF-8 sequences (e.g., latin-1 data), raises UnicodeDecodeError. In Python 2 no decode was needed.
patterns.python2_behavior: No decode needed — str and bytes were the same type
patterns.python3_behavior: bytes.decode() raises UnicodeDecodeError if content is not valid UTF-8
patterns.example_bug: text = data.decode()  # UnicodeDecodeError if data contains latin-1 bytes

patterns.description: str(b'hello') in Python 2 returns 'hello'. In Python 3, str(b'hello') returns "b'hello'" — the repr, not the content.
patterns.python2_behavior: str(b'data') returns 'data' — the content
patterns.python3_behavior: str(b'data') returns "b'data'" — the repr, with b-prefix and quotes included
patterns.example_bug: label = str(response_bytes)  # produces "b'...'" string in Python 3

patterns.description: % string formatting on bytes objects raises TypeError in Python 3. In Python 2, str was bytes so % formatting worked.
patterns.python2_behavior: b'Hello %s' % name works — str and bytes were the same
patterns.python3_behavior: TypeError: %b requires a bytes-like object, or object that implements __bytes__, not 'str'
patterns.example_bug: msg = b'Error: %s' % error_text  # TypeError in Python 3

patterns.description: Comparing a bytes variable to a str literal always returns False in Python 3 without raising any error. Most dangerous pattern — undetectable by any linter.
patterns.python2_behavior: b'OK' == 'OK' is True — bytes and str were interchangeable
patterns.python3_behavior: b'OK' == 'OK' is False — always, silently, with no warning or exception
patterns.example_bug: if response_code == 'OK': proceed()  # never executes when response_code is bytes

patterns.description: Comparing bytes to str with != always returns True in Python 3. Any guard using != to check bytes content silently passes when it should not.
patterns.python2_behavior: b'ERROR' != 'OK' is True — correct inequality check
patterns.python3_behavior: b'ERROR' != 'OK' is always True — even b'OK' != 'OK' is True, bypassing all equality guards
patterns.example_bug: if status != 'error': continue  # always continues, never catches error when status is bytes
